---
title: "ECOTS 2026 Breakout Session Proposal"
format: html
---

Submission instructions: <https://www.causeweb.org/cause/ecots/ecots26/proposals>

**Name**: Catalina Medina

**Institution**: California State University Channel Islands

**Co-Presenters (Optional)**: Alma Castro and Mine Dogucu

**Co-Presenters Institutions**: Cypress College and University of California, Irvine

**Primary Presenter's Email**: catalina.medina@csuci.edu

**Co-Presenter's Email**: acastro@cypresscollege.edu and mdogucu@uci.edu

**Session Title**: Teaching Introductory Data Science: Trial, Error, and Triumph for All

**Proposal Type**: 30 minute

**Brief Abstract (150/150 words)**:

Introduction to Data Science (IDS) instructors have to consider: what to teach, how to teach, what tools to use, and how to distinguish an IDS course from an introductory statistics course. This can especially be overwhelming for novice instructors of IDS. We have been there!!! In this breakout room, we will share our collective approach to teaching IDS at three different institutions: a research university, teaching university, and community college. Our approach to teaching data science involves a balance of statistics, computing, and domain science. We hope to spark joy in our classes by considering impacts of data science on humans and the world around us through accessibility, reproducibility, and ethical considerations. We will present an overview of the curriculum, demonstrate a classroom activity, and share examples from our new open-access IDS textbook, Hello Data Science, and its accompanying R package, hellodatascience, with real datasets from a variety of contexts.
 

**Connection to Theme**: 

To balance the impact of AI and technology broadly we highlight nontechnical aspects of data science on humans and the world around us through accessibility, reproducibility, and ethical considerations.
We think any instructor who has or might teach introductory data science can benefit from the resources we will share during the session and from connecting with each other.


**Engagement**:  

Polling software will be used instead of breakout rooms to have participants contribute throughout the fast-paced session. The session will begin with an introduction from the speakers and attendees including institution type and past experience teaching IDS. The speakers will then briefly present their curriculum and resources they've developed including discussion of an example classroom activity, their textbook [Hello Data Science](https://hellodata.science/), R package [hellodatascience](https://github.com/hellodata-science/hellodatascience), and lecture slides. The remainder of the session will be a Q \& A and discussion among participants. 


**Goal**: 

We want to provide an adaptable framework for educators that have not taught an introductory data science course before, and provide more resources to those that have.
